volumeTitle: Persians. Seven against Thebes. Suppliants. Prometheus Bound
label: Persians (Section 390 - Section 620)

sections.label: Section 390
sections.translation: clear from the island crags , an echo returned an answering cry . Terror fell on all the barbarians , balked of their purpose ; for then the Hellenes chanted their solemn paean , not as in flight , but as men rushing to the onset with the courage of gallant hearts .

sections.label: Section 395
sections.original: σάλπιγξ δ [*] ἀυτῇ πάντ ἐκεῖν ἐπέφλεγεν .
sections.translation: The trumpet with its blast set all their side afire , and instantly , at the word of command , with the even stroke of foaming oars they struck the briny deep . Swiftly they all came clear into view . Their right wing , well marshalled ,

sections.label: Section 400
sections.original: ἡγεῖτο κόσμῳ , δεύτερον δ ὁ πᾶς στόλος
sections.translation: led on in orderly advance , next their whole army pressed on against us , and at the same time a loud shout met our ears : On , you men of Hellas ! Free your native land . Free your children , your wives , the temples of your fathers ’ gods , and the tombs of your ancestors . Now you are fighting for all you have . Then from our side arose in response the mingled clamor of Persian speech , and straightaway the ships dashed together their bronze prows . It was a ship of Hellas

sections.label: Section 410
sections.translation: that began the charge and chopped off in its entirety the curved stern of a Phoenician boat . Each captain drove his ship straight against some other ship . At first the stream of the Persian army held its own . When , however , the mass of our ships had been crowded in the narrows , and none could render another aid ,

sections.label: Section 415
sections.original: αὐτοὶ θ [*] ὑφ [*] αὑτῶν ἐμβόλοις [*] χαλκοστόμοις
sections.translation: and each crashed its bronze prow against each of its own line , they splintered their whole bank of oars . Then the Hellenic galleys , not heedless of their chance , hemmed them in and battered them on every side . The hulls of our vessels rolled over , and the sea was hidden from our sight ,

sections.label: Section 420
sections.translation: strewn as it was with wrecks and slaughtered men . The shores and reefs were crowded with our dead , and every ship that formed a part of the barbarian fleet plied its oars in disorderly flight . But , as if our men were tuna or some haul of fish ,

sections.label: Section 425
sections.translation: the foe kept striking and hacking them with broken oars and fragments of wrecked ships . Groans and shrieks together filled the open sea until the face of black night hid the scene . But as for the the full extent of our disasters , this , even if I had ten days in succession to do so , I could not describe to you .

sections.label: Section 430
sections.translation: However , you can be sure that so great a multitude of men never perished in a single day .

sections.label: Section 433
sections.original: αἰαῖ , κακῶν δὴ πέλαγος ἔρρωγεν [*] μέγα
sections.translation: Alas ! In truth a vast sea of troubles has burst upon the Persians and the entire barbarian race .

sections.label: Section 435
sections.original: εὖ νυν [*] τόδ ἴσθι , μηδέπω μεσοῦν κακόν :
sections.translation: Be assured of this , not even half of the disaster has as yet been told . A calamity so dreadful as to outweigh these ills twice over befell them .

sections.label: Section 438
sections.original: καὶ τίς γένοιτ ἂν τῆσδ [*] ἔτ ἐχθίων τύχη ;
sections.translation: But what greater misfortune than this could have befallen them ? Speak ! What is this other disaster you say

sections.label: Section 440
sections.original: ἐλθεῖν κακῶν ῥέπουσαν ἐς τὰ μάσσονα .
sections.translation: came upon our force , sinking the scale to greater weight of ill ?

sections.label: Section 441
sections.translation: Those Persians who were in their life ’ s prime , bravest in spirit , pre-eminent for noble birth , and always among the foremost in loyalty to the King himself — these have fallen ignobly by a most inglorious doom .

sections.label: Section 445
sections.original: οἲ γὼ τάλαινα συμφορᾶς κακῆς , φίλοι .
sections.translation: Ah , I am truly reduced to misery through this disaster ! By what fate was it that you say they met their end ?

sections.label: Section 447
sections.original: νῆσός τις ἐστὶ πρόσθε Σαλαμῖνος τόπων ,
sections.translation: There is an island [*] lying before Salamis , a small one and dangerous anchorage for ships ; its sea-washed shore is the haunt of Pan , who loves the dance .

sections.label: Section 450
sections.original: ἐνταῦθα πέμπει τούσδ , ὅπως , [*] ὅταν νεῶν
sections.translation: There Xerxes dispatched these , his choicest troops , in order that when the Hellenic enemy , wrecked from their ships , should flee in search of safety to the island , they might slaughter their force , an easy prey , and rescue their comrades from the straits of the sea . Grievously did he misjudge the issue . For when some god

sections.label: Section 455
sections.translation: had given the glory to the Hellenes in the battle on the sea , on that same day , fencing their bodies in armor of bronze , they leapt from their ships and encircled the whole island , so that our men were at a loss which way to turn . Often they were struck by stones slung from their hands ,

sections.label: Section 460
sections.translation: and arrows sped from the bow-string kept falling upon them and doing them harm . At last the Hellenes , charging with one shout , struck them and hacked to pieces the limbs of the poor wretches , until they had utterly quenched the life of all .

sections.label: Section 465
sections.original: Ξέρξης δ ἀνῴμωξεν κακῶν ὁρῶν βάθος :
sections.translation: Xerxes groaned aloud when he beheld the extent of the disaster , for he occupied a seat commanding a clear view of the entire army — a lofty headland by the open sea . Tearing his robes and uttering a loud cry , he straightaway gave orders to his force on land

sections.label: Section 470
sections.original: ἵησ [*] ἀκόσμῳ ξὺν φυγῇ . τοιάνδε σοι
sections.translation: and dismissed them in disorderly flight . This , besides the one already told , is the disaster you must bewail .

sections.label: Section 472
sections.original: ὦ στυγνὲ δαῖμον , ὡς ἄρ ἔψευσας φρενῶν
sections.translation: O hateful divinity , how have you foiled the purpose of the Persians ! Cruel was the vengeance which my son brought upon himself for his designs against illustrious Athens ; the barbarians

sections.label: Section 475
sections.translation: whom Marathon destroyed were not enough . It was in an effort to exact retribution for them that my son has drawn upon himself so great a multitude of woes . But the ships that escaped destruction — tell me about them . Where did you leave them ? Can you give a clear report ?

sections.label: Section 480
sections.original: ναῶν γε [*] ταγοὶ τῶν λελειμμένων σύδην
sections.translation: The commanders of the ships which still remained fled with a rush in disorder wherever the wind bore them . As for the survivors of the army , they perished in Boeotian territory , some , faint from thirst , beside a refreshing spring , while some of us , exhausted and panting ,

sections.label: Section 485
sections.translation: made our way to the land of the Phocians , to Doris and the Melian gulf , where the Spercheus waters the plain with kindly stream . Coming from there , badly in need of food , we received welcome in the Achaean land and the cities of the Thessalians .

sections.label: Section 490
sections.original: βορᾶς ἐδέξαντ : ἔνθα δὴ πλεῖστοι θάνον
sections.translation: There it was that many perished of thirst and hunger , for we were oppressed by both . And we came to the Magnesian land and to the country of the Macedonians , to the ford of the Axius and Bolbe ’ s reedy marsh , and to Mount Pangaeus ,

sections.label: Section 495
sections.original: Ἠδωνίδ αἶαν : νυκτὶ δ ἐν ταύτῃ θεὸς
sections.translation: in the Edonian land . But on that night the god roused winter before its time and froze the stream of sacred Strymon from shore to shore . Many a man who before that had held the gods in no esteem , implored them then in supplication , doing obeisance to earth and heaven .

sections.label: Section 500
sections.translation: But when our host had made an end of its fervent invocation of the gods , it ventured to pass across the ice-bound stream . And each of us who started on his way before the sun god dispersed his beams , found himself in safety , for the bright orb of the sun with its burning rays

sections.label: Section 505
sections.translation: heated the middle section and pierced it with its flames . One after another our men sank in , and fortunate indeed was he who perished soonest . The survivors , after making their way through Thrace with great hardship ,

sections.label: Section 510
sections.translation: — and few they were indeed — escaped to the safety of the land of their homes ; now the city of the Persians may make lament in regret for the beloved youth of the land . What I say is true , yet much remains untold of the ills launched by Heaven upon the Persians . Exit

sections.label: Section 515
sections.original: ὦ δυσπόνητε δαῖμον , ὡς ἄγαν βαρὺς
sections.translation: O unearthly power , source of our cruel distress , with what crushing weight have you fallen upon the whole Persian race !

sections.label: Section 517
sections.translation: How the utter destruction of our host distresses me ! O vivid vision of my dreams at night , how clearly did you signify misfortune to me !

sections.label: Section 520
sections.original: ὑμεῖς δὲ φαύλως αὔτ ἄγαν ἐκρίνατε .
sections.translation: And all too lightly did you in turn interpret it . However , since your explanation determined thus , first of all I wish to offer prayers to the gods , and then I will return after I have brought from the palace a sacrificial cake as a gift to Earth and the dead .

sections.label: Section 525
sections.translation: I know indeed that it is for what cannot be undone , yet I do this in the hope that something more auspicious may come to pass in the future . But you should confer faithfully with the faithful counsellors in view of what has befallen . And as for my son , if he should come here before I return ,

sections.label: Section 530
sections.translation: comfort him and escort him to the palace , so that he will not inflict on himself some further ill to crown those already ours . Exit

sections.label: Section 532
sections.original: ὦ Ζεῦ βασιλεῦ , νῦν μὲν [*] Περσῶν
sections.translation: O sovereign Zeus , by destroying the army of the haughty and multitudinous Persians ,

sections.label: Section 535
sections.translation: you have shrouded in the gloom of grief the city of Susa and of Agbatana ! Many a woman , who has a share in this sorrow , tears her veil with tender hands

sections.label: Section 540
sections.translation: and moistens with drenching tears the robe covering her bosom . And the Persian wives , indulging in soft wailing through longing to behold their lords and abandoning the daintily wrought coverlets of their couches , the delight of their youth ,

sections.label: Section 545
sections.translation: mourn with complainings that know no end . So I too sustain the truly woeful fate of those who are gone .

sections.label: Section 1.548
sections.translation: For now in truth the whole land of Asia , decimated , moans :

sections.label: Section 1.550
sections.translation: Xerxes led forth ( woe !), Xerxes laid low ( woe !), Xerxes disposed all things imprudently with his sea-going vessels . Why then was Darius

sections.label: Section 1.555
sections.translation: in his time so unscathed by disaster , he who was ruler of archers , to the men of Susa a beloved leader ?

sections.label: Section 1.558
sections.translation: For infantry and seamen both , the ships , dark-eyed [*] and linen-winged ,

sections.label: Section 1.560
sections.translation: led forth ( woe !), the ships laid them low ( woe !), the ships , under the deadly impact of the foe and by the hands of Ionians .

sections.label: Section 1.565
sections.translation: The King himself , as we learn , has barely made his escape over the wintry paths which traverse the plains of Thrace .

sections.label: Section 2.568
sections.translation: And they who were first to meet their doom ( alas !), left behind by dire necessity ( alas !),

sections.label: Section 2.570
sections.translation: are swept along the Cychrean strand ( woe !). Groan and gnash your teeth ; in grievous strain shout forth our woes till they reach the heavens ( alas !), raise high

sections.label: Section 2.575
sections.translation: your wailing clamor in cries of misery .

sections.label: Section 2.576
sections.translation: Lacerated by the swirling waters ( alas !) they are gnawed ( alas !) by the voiceless children of the undefiled sea ( alas !). The home , bereaved of his presence , laments its head ;

sections.label: Section 2.580
sections.translation: and parents , bereft of their children , in their old age bewail their heaven- sent woes ( alas !), now that they learn the full measure of their afflictions .

sections.label: Section 3.584
sections.original: τοὶ δ ἀνὰ γᾶν Ἀσίαν δὴν
sections.translation: Not now for long will those who dwell throughout the length and breadth of Asia

sections.label: Section 3.585
sections.translation: abide under the sway of the Persians , nor will they pay further tribute at the compulsion of their lord , nor will they prostrate themselves to the earth and do him reverence ;

sections.label: Section 3.590
sections.translation: for the royal power has perished utterly .

sections.label: Section 3.591
sections.translation: No longer will men keep a curb upon their tongues ; for the people are set free to utter their thoughts at will , now that the yoke of power has been broken .

sections.label: Section 3.595
sections.translation: The blood-stained soil of Ajax ’ sea-washed isle holds all that once was Persia .

sections.label: Section 598
sections.original: φίλοι , κακῶν μὲν ὅστις ἔμπειρος [*] κυρεῖ ,
sections.translation: My friends , whoever has experience of misery knows that when a sea

sections.label: Section 600
sections.translation: of troubles comes upon mortal men , they view all things with alarm ; but when fortune flows with prosperous tide , they believe that this same fortune will forever bring them success . Now in my case everything seems full of dread ; before my eyes appears the enmity of the gods ,

sections.label: Section 605
sections.original: βοᾷ δ ἐν ὠσὶ κέλαδος οὐ παιώνιος :
sections.translation:
and in my ears there rings a sound that has no note of joy ; such is my consternation at the evil tidings which terrify my soul .
It is for this reason that I have come here from the palace once again , without my chariot and my former pomp , and bring , as propitiatory libations for the father of my son ,

sections.label: Section 610
sections.translation: offerings that soothe the dead , both white milk , pleasant to drink , from an unblemished cow , and bright honey , distillation wrought from blossoms by the bee , together with lustral water from a virgin spring ;

sections.label: Section 615
sections.translation:
and from a rustic source , this unmixed draught , the quickening juice of an ancient vine . Here too is the fragrant fruit of the pale-green olive that lives the entirety of its life in luxuriant foliage ; and garlanded flowers , produce of the bounteous earth .
But come , my friends ,

sections.label: Section 620
sections.translation: chant solemn songs as I make these libations to the dead , and summon forth the divine spirit of Darius , while I convey , in honor of the gods , these offerings for the earth to drink .
